sources.title: Grade 8 - Term 3: The Scramble for Africa: late 19th century
Key terms: The Scramble for Africa - late 19th century
General Act of the Berlin Conference on West Africa, 26 February 1885
The Partition of Africa 1880-1900 and European Imperialism in the Nineteenth Century by John M. MacKenzie
Glen Grey Act (The Native Issue) by Cecil John Rhodes, 30 July 1894
The class consequences of colonialism in Africa by Richard D. Wolff

slides.heading: The Scramble for Africa: Late 19th Century
slides.lead: How, in just a few decades, European powers seized almost the whole of Africa - and how Africans fought back.

image.alt: An outline of the African continent overlaid with straight dividing lines and small flags, showing the continent being carved up.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 200' role='img'><rect width='320' height='200' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M150 24 q40 4 46 30 q10 30 -6 46 q6 26 -10 44 q-8 26 -30 34 q-18 -4 -22 -26 q-24 -10 -28 -40 q-14 -20 -4 -44 q10 -30 54 -44 Z' fill='#c8b89a' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='96' y1='70' x2='202' y2='70' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='24' x2='150' y2='178' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='110' y1='120' x2='196' y2='120' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='118' y='48' width='14' height='10' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='168' y='88' width='14' height='10' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect x='120' y='136' width='14' height='10' fill='#3b5a8a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open by asking learners what they think 'scramble' means. Explain the big idea: between about 1880 and 1900 European countries competed to grab African land and resources, and by 1914 they controlled almost the entire continent. Preview the three threads of the lesson: why Europe wanted Africa, how they divided it at the Berlin Conference, and how Africans resisted.

slides.heading: Africa before the Scramble
slides.lead: By the mid-1800s Africa was home to hundreds of independent societies, kingdoms and empires.

blocks.text: Before about 1880, Europeans mostly traded along Africa's coasts and had few colonies inland. Africa had its own **states, trade routes, languages and religions**, from the Sokoto Caliphate in West Africa to the Zulu Kingdom in the south and the ancient empire of Ethiopia.

items: Most of the continent was ruled by Africans, not Europeans.
African societies traded gold, ivory, cloth, salt and other goods over long distances.
Europeans knew the coasts well but little of the interior.

slides.notes: Correct a common misconception: Africa was not 'empty' or 'waiting to be discovered'. It had complex, organised societies with their own governments and economies. This matters because it frames the Scramble as an invasion of existing nations, not the settling of empty land.

slides.heading: Why did Europe want Africa?
slides.lead: The Scramble had economic, political and social causes that pushed on one another.

blocks.heading: Raw materials
blocks.text: Europe's factories (from the Industrial Revolution) needed rubber, cotton, copper, gold and diamonds.

blocks.heading: New markets
blocks.text: Colonies gave European industries places to sell their manufactured goods.

blocks.heading: National rivalry
blocks.text: Britain, France and Germany competed for power and prestige - owning colonies showed a country was 'great'.

blocks.heading: Ideas and religion
blocks.text: Some Europeans claimed a 'civilising mission' to spread Christianity - beliefs shaped by racist ideas of European superiority.

slides.notes: Link back to the Grade 8 Term 1 topic on the Industrial Revolution: industry created hunger for raw materials and markets, which drove the Scramble. Be explicit that the 'civilising mission' was used to justify conquest and rested on racist ideas that Europeans were superior - not an accurate or fair description of Africa.

slides.heading: What made rapid conquest possible?
slides.lead: New technology and medicine tipped the balance of power towards European armies.

blocks.text: For centuries disease had kept Europeans out of Africa's interior. In the 1800s new inventions changed that. The drug **quinine** protected against malaria, **steamships** carried soldiers up rivers, and railways and the telegraph moved troops and messages quickly.

items: The Maxim machine gun gave European forces deadly firepower against African armies.
Quinine let European soldiers survive in malaria zones.
Steamships, railways and the telegraph made it easier to control distant land.

image.alt: A steamship, a length of railway track and a telegraph pole shown side by side as symbols of new technology.

slides.notes: Stress the technology gap: African armies were often large and brave, but the Maxim gun and modern rifles gave a huge advantage. This helps learners understand HOW a small number of Europeans could conquer such vast areas - it was not because African societies were weak or unwilling to fight.

slides.heading: The Berlin Conference
slides.lead: European powers met in Berlin to agree rules for dividing Africa among themselves - and no Africans were invited.

blocks.text: As European countries raced to claim African land, their rivalry threatened to cause war between them. The German leader **Otto von Bismarck** called a conference in **Berlin from November 1884 to February 1885**. Representatives of about 14 European nations (and the United States) attended.

items: The aim was to divide Africa peacefully - for the Europeans, not for Africans.
Not a single African ruler was invited to the conference.
The meeting ended with the signing of the General Act on 26 February 1885.

image.alt: A group of figures seated around a conference table with a map of Africa in the centre.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 150' role='img'><rect width='240' height='150' fill='#f4efe6'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='92' rx='78' ry='30' fill='#7a5a3a'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='88' rx='48' ry='18' fill='#dcd3c2'/><path d='M112 78 q10 1 12 8 q2 8 -2 12 q-6 6 -14 4 q-6 -2 -6 -10 q0 -8 10 -14 Z' fill='#c8b89a'/><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='60' cy='60' r='7'/><circle cx='96' cy='52' r='7'/><circle cx='144' cy='52' r='7'/><circle cx='180' cy='60' r='7'/><circle cx='60' cy='118' r='7'/><circle cx='120' cy='126' r='7'/><circle cx='180' cy='118' r='7'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This is the key event of the topic. Make two points firmly: (1) the conference was about avoiding war BETWEEN European powers, not about Africa's people; (2) the total exclusion of Africans meant decisions about their land were made without them. This is why historians see the Berlin Conference as a symbol of the whole Scramble.

slides.heading: The rules of the partition
slides.lead: The Berlin Conference set out how a European country could claim a piece of Africa.

blocks.text: The General Act laid down guidelines for claiming territory. The most important idea was **'effective occupation'**: to claim land, a European power had to show it actually controlled it, with officials, soldiers or treaties. This encouraged a rush to occupy as much land as possible.

items: A power claiming coastline also had to notify the others of its claim.
The Congo River basin was declared a free-trade zone open to all European traders.
Borders were often drawn as straight lines on a map, ignoring African communities on the ground.

image.alt: A map fragment with a straight boundary line cutting across rivers and a village symbol.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 150' role='img'><rect width='240' height='150' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='20' y='20' width='90' height='110' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='110' y='20' width='110' height='110' fill='#c8b89a'/><line x1='110' y1='20' x2='110' y2='130' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M20 60 q40 20 90 0 q40 -18 90 0' fill='none' stroke='#3b5a8a' stroke-width='3'/><g fill='#5c1414'><rect x='96' y='90' width='12' height='10'/><rect x='114' y='90' width='12' height='10'/></g><path d='M96 90 l6 -8 l6 8 Z' fill='#5c1414'/><path d='M114 90 l6 -8 l6 8 Z' fill='#5c1414'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain 'effective occupation' in simple terms: you only kept the land if you could prove you controlled it, so powers rushed to plant flags and sign treaties with (or force treaties on) African leaders. Highlight the human cost of straight-line borders: single communities were split between two colonies, and rival groups were forced together - a problem that still affects Africa's borders today.

slides.heading: Carving up the continent
slides.lead: By 1914 almost all of Africa was ruled by a handful of European powers.

blocks.text: In just a few decades the map of Africa was redrawn. **Britain and France** took the largest shares, while **Germany, Belgium, Portugal, Italy and Spain** claimed other territories. Only **Ethiopia and Liberia** kept their independence.

items: Britain dreamed of a line of colonies 'from Cairo to Cape Town'.
France controlled much of West and North Africa.
King Leopold II of Belgium took the Congo as his personal possession.

image.alt: A simplified map of Africa shaded into several coloured blocks, each standing for a different European power.

slides.notes: Use a real historical map alongside this simplified one if you have it. The point is the speed and scale: an entire continent divided in roughly one generation. Note that Ethiopia's independence links forward to the Battle of Adwa (a later slide), and that Liberia had been founded by freed people from the United States.

slides.heading: The Congo under King Leopold II
slides.lead: The Congo shows how brutal colonial rule could be when profit came before people.

blocks.text: King **Leopold II of Belgium** ran the Congo as his private property from 1885. To harvest **rubber and ivory** for huge profits, his officials forced Congolese people to work under a system of violence and cruel punishment. Millions of people are believed to have died from violence, hunger and disease.

items: Villages were forced to collect rubber or face harsh punishment.
News of the abuses eventually caused an outcry in Europe.
In 1908 the Belgian government took control away from Leopold.

image.alt: A rubber tree with a bucket collecting sap, symbolising forced labour in the Congo.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='92' y='40' width='16' height='96' fill='#5c4a33'/><circle cx='100' cy='42' r='30' fill='#2f5233'/><circle cx='74' cy='52' r='18' fill='#3f6b45'/><circle cx='126' cy='52' r='18' fill='#3f6b45'/><path d='M100 70 q10 20 -2 34' fill='none' stroke='#dcd3c2' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M84 120 h24 l-4 16 h-16 Z' fill='#7a5a3a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Handle this slide sensitively and factually - it is one of the most severe examples of colonial violence. Keep the language age-appropriate: describe forced labour and its terrible human cost without graphic detail. The lesson is that the drive for profit under colonial rule caused enormous suffering, and that people in Europe and Africa eventually spoke out against it. Death-toll figures for the Congo are debated among historians.

slides.heading: The Scramble at a glance, 1880-1908

blocks.alt: A timeline of key events in the Scramble for Africa from 1880 to 1908.

events.label: European powers race to claim African land
General Act signed; Congo becomes Leopold's
Ethiopia defeats Italy at the Battle of Adwa
Herero and Nama resistance in South West Africa
Maji Maji uprising in German East Africa
Belgian state takes the Congo from Leopold

slides.notes: Use this slide to consolidate the sequence before moving from 'partition' to 'resistance'. Ask learners to notice that resistance (Adwa, Herero and Nama, Maji Maji) came quickly after conquest - Africans did not simply accept colonial rule. The next slides look at that resistance.

slides.heading: Africans fought back
slides.lead: Across the continent, African societies resisted colonial takeover in many different ways.

blocks.text: Colonial rule was not accepted quietly. Some African leaders fought wars of resistance, others used **diplomacy and treaties** to protect their people, and many ordinary people resisted through **strikes, refusing to pay taxes and keeping their cultures alive**.

items: Armed resistance: kingdoms and communities took up arms against invading armies.
Diplomatic resistance: some rulers negotiated to slow or limit European control.
Everyday resistance: people resisted through protest, non-cooperation and culture.

slides.notes: Broaden learners' picture of resistance beyond famous battles. Resistance included not only warfare but also diplomacy, tax boycotts, migration and cultural survival. Stress that resistance was widespread even though most armed struggles were eventually defeated by superior European firepower - the following two slides give concrete case studies.

slides.heading: The Battle of Adwa, Ethiopia
slides.lead: Ethiopia defeated an invading Italian army and kept its independence.

blocks.text: In 1896, Italy tried to conquer Ethiopia. Emperor **Menelik II** united Ethiopian forces and, at the **Battle of Adwa on 1 March 1896**, decisively defeated the Italian army. Ethiopia remained one of the only African states to keep its independence through the Scramble.

items: Menelik II had built up a large, well-armed and united force.
The victory forced Italy to recognise Ethiopia's independence.
Adwa became a proud symbol of African resistance across the continent.

image.alt: A raised flag on a hilltop with figures standing in victory, representing the Ethiopian success at Adwa.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M0 120 q60 -40 110 -10 q50 30 110 0 v50 H0 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><line x1='120' y1='40' x2='120' y2='120' stroke='#5c4a33' stroke-width='4'/><rect x='120' y='42' width='46' height='30' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='120' y='52' width='46' height='10' fill='#e8a13a'/><g fill='#5c1414'><circle cx='70' cy='104' r='8'/><rect x='64' y='112' width='12' height='22'/><circle cx='96' cy='100' r='8'/><rect x='90' y='108' width='12' height='24'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Adwa is essential for balance: it shows that Africans could and did defeat European armies. Explain why it succeeded - unity under Menelik II, modern weapons, and knowledge of the land. It inspired anti-colonial movements far beyond Ethiopia. Use it to counter any impression that European victory was inevitable.

slides.heading: Maji Maji, Herero and Nama
slides.lead: Two major uprisings against German rule show both the courage of resistance and the brutal cost.

blocks.text: In **German East Africa** (today Tanzania), the **Maji Maji uprising (1905-1907)** united many communities against forced cotton-growing and harsh rule. In **German South West Africa** (today Namibia), the **Herero and Nama people rose up in 1904**. German forces crushed both, and their treatment of the Herero and Nama is today recognised as one of the first **genocides** of the 20th century.

items: Maji Maji united different groups against a shared enemy.
The Herero and Nama were driven into the desert and killed in huge numbers.
These events show both the strength of resistance and the cruelty of colonial rule.

slides.notes: Handle this slide with care - it deals with mass killing. At Grade 8 level, name the genocide honestly but avoid graphic detail. Emphasise two ideas: the bravery and unity of the resistance (especially Maji Maji drawing many groups together), and the terrible consequences colonial powers were willing to impose. This is a serious, sensitive topic that rewards a calm, respectful classroom discussion. Specific casualty figures vary between sources.

slides.heading: The lasting effects of the Scramble
slides.lead: The partition of Africa reshaped the continent in ways still felt today.

blocks.text: By 1914 nearly all of Africa was under European control. African peoples lost their independence and land, and colonial economies were built to serve Europe. The **borders drawn in Berlin** cut across communities and became the borders of many modern African countries.

items: Africans lost political independence and control of their own resources.
Colonial borders ignored African communities and still cause tension today.
The struggle against colonial rule would grow into 20th-century independence movements.

slides.notes: Connect past to present: many of Africa's current borders, and some of its conflicts, trace back to lines drawn by Europeans who never set foot in those places. End on continuity and change - the resistance of the 1890s and 1900s laid foundations for the independence movements learners will study later. In the South African context, link to how colonial and settler control shaped the road to Union and apartheid.

slides.heading: What we learned
slides.lead: The Scramble for Africa was the rapid European conquest of Africa - resisted bravely, with consequences that still matter.

blocks.text: In roughly one generation, European powers divided and conquered almost all of Africa, agreeing the rules among themselves at the **Berlin Conference of 1884-1885** while excluding Africans entirely. Africans resisted through war, diplomacy and everyday defiance - winning at **Adwa** and rising up in **Maji Maji** and against German rule - but most were defeated by superior firepower, leaving deep and lasting effects.

blocks.question: Explain two reasons why European countries wanted to colonise Africa in the late 19th century.
Why is the Berlin Conference of 1884-1885 seen as an important turning point? Refer to who attended and who was left out.
Describe one example of African resistance to colonial rule and explain what it shows us.

slides.notes: Use the three questions as a written task or class discussion; the mark allocations mirror CAPS source-based and extended-writing styles. Remind learners of the balance in this topic: European conquest was rapid and often brutal, but Africans were active agents who resisted in many ways. This sets up later units on 20th-century African independence and on South Africa's own path through Union and apartheid.
